review_comments.author: Andrew Morton
review_comments.summary: reviewer noted that the patch introduces a small overhead in terms of text size and cache footprint, questioning whether this added complexity is justified by the benefits of improved maintainability
review_comments.raw_body:
guard() is cute, but this patch adds a little overhead - defconfig
page_alloc.o text increases by 32 bytes, presumably all in
reserve_highatomic_pageblock().  More instructions, larger cache
footprint.

So we're adding a little overhead to every user's Linux machine for all
time.  In return for which the developers get a little convenience and
maintainability.

Is it worth it?

review_comments.author: Steven Rostedt
review_comments.summary:
reviewer noted that zone lock guards are not properly ordered with other locks in the mm functions, specifically mentioning that __offline_isolated_pages() holds the zone lock while also holding the page lock, and requested a reordering of the locks to fix this issue

reviewer questioned whether the introduction of zone lock guards would result in a measurable performance slowdown, and requested data on this aspect

reviewer suggested that the zone lock guard approach be reconsidered in favor of improving the generic locking infrastructure, potentially for performance reasons

review_comments.author: tip-bot2 Zijlstra
review_comments.summary: The reviewer noted that the zone lock guard introduced in this patch increases code size unnecessarily due to an unconditional __GUARD_IS_ERR() call, similar to IS_ERR_OR_NULL(), which generates sub-optimal code.
review_comments.raw_body:
The following commit has been merged into the locking/core branch of tip:

Commit-ID:     2deccd5c862a0337a691bcfaa87919b4216e6103
Gitweb:        https://git.kernel.org/tip/2deccd5c862a0337a691bcfaa87919b4216e6103
Author:        Peter Zijlstra <peterz@infradead.org>
AuthorDate:    Mon, 09 Mar 2026 17:40:42 +01:00
Committer:     Peter Zijlstra <peterz@infradead.org>
CommitterDate: Mon, 16 Mar 2026 13:16:49 +01:00

cleanup: Optimize guards

Andrew reported that a guard() conversion of zone_lock increased the
code size unnecessarily.

It turns out the unconditional __GUARD_IS_ERR() is to blame. As
explored earlier [1], __GUARD_IS_ERR(), similar to IS_ERR_OR_NULL(),
generates somewhat sub-optimal code.

However, looking at things again, it is possible to avoid doing the
__GUARD_IS_ERR() unconditionally. Revert the normal destructors to a
simple NULL test and only add the IS_ERR bit to COND guards.

This cures the reported overhead; as compiled by GCC-16:

	page_alloc.o:

pre:	Total: Before=45299, After=45371, chg +0.16%
post:	Total: Before=45299, After=45026, chg -0.60%

[1] https://lkml.kernel.org/r/20250513085001.GC25891@noisy.programming.kicks-ass.net

Reported-by: Andrew Morton <akpm@linux-foundation.org>
Signed-off-by: Peter Zijlstra (Intel) <peterz@infradead.org>
Tested-by: Dan Williams <dan.j.williams@intel.com>
Link: https://patch.msgid.link/20260309164516.GE606826@noisy.programming.kicks-ass.net

 1 file changed, 11 insertions(+), 8 deletions(-)

index dbc4162..ea95ca4 100644

 	__no_context_analysis						\
 { _type t = _init; return t; }
 
-#define EXTEND_CLASS(_name, ext, _init, _init_args...)			\
-typedef lock_##_name##_t lock_##_name##ext##_t;			\
+#define EXTEND_CLASS_COND(_name, ext, _cond, _init, _init_args...)	\
+typedef lock_##_name##_t lock_##_name##ext##_t;				\
 typedef class_##_name##_t class_##_name##ext##_t;			\
-static __always_inline void class_##_name##ext##_destructor(class_##_name##_t *p) \
-{ class_##_name##_destructor(p); }					\
+static __always_inline void class_##_name##ext##_destructor(class_##_name##_t *_T) \
+{ if (_cond) return; class_##_name##_destructor(_T); }			\
 static __always_inline class_##_name##_t class_##_name##ext##_constructor(_init_args) \
 	__no_context_analysis \
 { class_##_name##_t t = _init; return t; }
 
+#define EXTEND_CLASS(_name, ext, _init, _init_args...)			\
+	EXTEND_CLASS_COND(_name, ext, 0, _init, _init_args)
+
 #define CLASS(_name, var)						\
 	class_##_name##_t var __cleanup(class_##_name##_destructor) =	\
 		class_##_name##_constructor
@@ -394,12 +397,12 @@ static __maybe_unused const bool class_##_name##_is_conditional = _is_cond
 	__DEFINE_GUARD_LOCK_PTR(_name, _T)
 
 #define DEFINE_GUARD(_name, _type, _lock, _unlock) \
-	DEFINE_CLASS(_name, _type, if (!__GUARD_IS_ERR(_T)) { _unlock; }, ({ _lock; _T; }), _type _T); \
+	DEFINE_CLASS(_name, _type, if (_T) { _unlock; }, ({ _lock; _T; }), _type _T); \
 	DEFINE_CLASS_IS_GUARD(_name)
 
 #define DEFINE_GUARD_COND_4(_name, _ext, _lock, _cond) \

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 1/8] mm: use zone lock guard in reserve_highatomic_pageblock()

version_history.subject: [PATCH 1/8] mm: use zone lock guard in reserve_highatomic_pageblock()

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch updates the reserve_highatomic_pageblock() function in the Linux kernel's page allocation code to use a zone lock guard instead of explicit lock/unlock and goto out_unlock patterns. The zone lock guard is a new feature introduced by this patch, which provides automatic scope-based cleanup when exiting the locked region. This change simplifies the code and reduces the risk of errors due to forgotten unlocks or incorrect locking orders. The patch also updates the include/linux/mmzone_lock.h file to define the zone lock guard and its attributes.

review_comments.author: Andrew Morton
review_comments.summary: The reviewer noted that the patch introduces additional overhead in reserve_highatomic_pageblock() due to the use of the zone_lock_irqsave lock guard, resulting in a 32-byte increase in page_alloc.o text size, and questioned whether this added complexity is justified.
review_comments.raw_body:
guard() is cute, but this patch adds a little overhead - defconfig
page_alloc.o text increases by 32 bytes, presumably all in
reserve_highatomic_pageblock().  More instructions, larger cache
footprint.

So we're adding a little overhead to every user's Linux machine for all
time.  In return for which the developers get a little convenience and
maintainability.

Is it worth it?

review_comments.author: Steven Rostedt
review_comments.summary:
reviewer pointed out that the zone_lock_irqsave lock guard does not properly handle interrupts, suggesting to use a different locking mechanism

Reviewer Steven Rostedt questioned whether using a zone lock guard would introduce a measurable performance slowdown, and asked if maintainability was the sole concern.

reviewer suggested improving the performance of the zone lock guard mechanism, which is being used in multiple kernel locations
review_comments.raw_body:
[ Adding Peter ]

---

I think maintainability is of importance. Is there any measurable slowdown?
Or are we only worried about the text size increase?

---

This is being done all over the kernel. Perhaps we should look at ways to
make the generic infrastructure more performant?

-- Steve

reviewer noted that the patch introduces a performance regression due to increased function call overhead, citing a perf tool output showing an increase in get_page_from_freelist() calls

Reviewer Vlastimil Babka questioned whether the zone lock guard is actually doing anything different from the original explicit lock/unlock pattern, suggesting that either there's an issue with the infrastructure or compiler heuristics are at play.

of compiler heuristics. In both cases imho worth looking into rather than
rejecting the construct.

CommitterDate: Mon, 16 Mar 2026 13:16:49 +01:00

cleanup: Optimize guards

Andrew reported that a guard() conversion of zone_lock increased the
code size unnecessarily.

It turns out the unconditional __GUARD_IS_ERR() is to blame. As
explored earlier [1], __GUARD_IS_ERR(), similar to IS_ERR_OR_NULL(),
generates somewhat sub-optimal code.

However, looking at things again, it is possible to avoid doing the
__GUARD_IS_ERR() unconditionally. Revert the normal destructors to a
simple NULL test and only add the IS_ERR bit to COND guards.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v4 5/6] NFSD: Add export-scoped state revocation
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch addresses an issue where NFS state revocation is not properly scoped to the intended export, potentially affecting multiple directories due to hard-linked files. The problem arises from the current implementation's inability to accurately determine whether a file's state should be revoked based on its export scope. To fix this, the patch proposes adding a new field (st_export) to track the export association for each state ID.

patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch delays marking a folio dirty until the end of a copy operation, potentially reducing overhead by avoiding unnecessary checks for page dirtiness.

review_comments.author: Bernd Schubert
review_comments.summary:
reviewer questioned the necessity of adding an extra folio_test_dirty() check, suggesting it may introduce unnecessary overhead for large folios

Reviewer noted that unlock_request() and set_page_dirty_lock() take significant CPU time on Intel CPUs, comparable to memcpy operations, and suggested iterating over pages to set dirty flag once per folio to reduce overhead.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Joanne,

On 3/18/26 22:19, Joanne Koong wrote:

---

a little bit background here. We see in cpu flame graphs that the spin
lock taken in unlock_request() and unlock_request() takes about the same
amount of CPU time as the memcpy. Interestingly, only on Intel, but not
AMD CPUs. Note that we are running with out custom page pinning, which
just takes the pages from an array, so iov_iter_get_pages2() is not used.

The reason for that unlock/lock is documented at the end of
Documentation/filesystems/fuse/fuse.rst as Kamikaze file system. Well we
don't have that, so for now these checks are modified in our branches to
avoid the lock. Although that is not upstreamable. Right solution is
here to extract an array of pages and do that unlock/lock per pagevec.

Next in the flame graph is setting that set_page_dirty_lock which also
takes as much CPU time as the memcpy. Again, Intel CPUs only.
In the combination with the above pagevec method, I think right solution
is to iterate over the pages, stores the last folio and then set to
dirty once per folio.
Also, I disagree about that the userspace buffers are not likely large
folios, see commit
59ba47b6be9cd0146ef9a55c6e32e337e11e7625 "fuse: Check for large folio)
with SPLICE_F_MOVE". Especially Horst persistently runs into it when
doing xfstests with recent kernels. I think the issue came up first time
with 3.18ish.

One can further enforce that by setting
"/sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/enabled" to 'always', what I did
when I tested the above commit. And actually that points out that
libfuse allocations should do the madvise. I'm going to do that during
the next days, maybe tomorrow.


Thanks,
Bernd

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author asked for clarification on whether the issue can be reproduced, indicating a lack of concrete evidence to support their previous claim that the extra folio_test_dirty() check adds overhead.

Author Joanne Koong responded to a concern about the patch's performance impact, explaining that the extra overhead of folio_test_dirty() check is likely negligible compared to other IO operations and context switches. She acknowledged that the issue can be reproducible in specific configurations but believes it's unlikely to occur on production workloads.
review_comments.raw_body:
Thanks for the background context. The intel vs amd difference is
interesting. The approaches you mention sound reasonable. Are you able
to share the flame graph or is this easily repro-able using fio on the
passthrough_hp server?

---

I think that's because xfstests uses /tmp for scratch space, so the

    "This is easily reproducible (on 6.19) with
    CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE_SHMEM_HUGE_ALWAYS=y
    CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE_TMPFS_HUGE_ALWAYS=y"

triggers it but on production workloads I don't think it's likely that
those source pages are backed by shmem/tmpfs or exist in the page
cache already as a large folio as the server has no control over that.
I also don't think most applications use splice, though maybe I'm
wrong here.

For non-splice, even if the user sets
"/sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/enabled" to 'always' or in
libfuse we do madvise on the buffer allocation for huge pages, that
has a 2 MB granularity requirement which depends on the user system
also having explicitly upped the max pages limit through the sysctl
since the kernel fuse max pages limit is 256 (1 MB) by default. I
don't think that is common on most servers.

Thanks,
Joanne

review_comments.author: Darrick Wong
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Darrick Wong noted that xfs can create large folios in its pagecache, which may interact negatively with the patch's extra folio_test_dirty() check.
review_comments.raw_body:
/me stumbles in-thread to note that xfs gets large folios for its files'
pagecache fairly frequently now, especially as readahead ramps up.

Ok back to the hell that is deploying ClownStrike through a Java program
while Firefox repeatedly drives my laptop to OOM.

--D

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 1/7] mm: list_lru: lock_list_lru_of_memcg() cannot return NULL if !skip_empty
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series aims to simplify the locking mechanism in list_lru.c by removing unnecessary NULL checks when acquiring locks for list additions and deletions. The code currently walks up the cgroup hierarchy until a valid lock is found, making the NULL checks redundant.

review_comments.author: Shakeel Butt
review_comments.summary: Approved the patch with Acked-by, indicating no objections or concerns.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Wed, Mar 18, 2026 at 03:53:19PM -0400, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> skip_empty is only for the shrinker to abort and skip a list that's
> empty or whose cgroup is being deleted.
> 
> For list additions and deletions, the cgroup hierarchy is walked
> upwards until a valid list_lru head is found, or it will fall back to
> the node list. Acquiring the lock won't fail. Remove the NULL checks
> in those callers.
> 
> Reviewed-by: David Hildenbrand (Arm) <david@kernel.org>
> Signed-off-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>

Acked-by: Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev>

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 5/7] mm: list_lru: introduce caller locking for additions and deletions
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces caller locking for additions and deletions in the list_lru API, allowing callers to synchronize auxiliary state with LRU state. The THP shrinker is a specific use case that requires this feature. Normal and irqsafe locking primitives are provided as well as caller-locked variants of addition and deletion functions. This change enables the THP shrinker to switch from its custom LRU to list_lru.

If we move __list_lru_walk_one to use irq_flags then we can remove the irq
param. It is reclaim code path and I don't think additional cost of irqsave
would matter here.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 6/7] mm: list_lru: introduce folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a helper function, folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc(), to allocate list_lru heads for memcg-aware objects coming from a folio. The function aims to make the existence check lightweight under RCU and only pin the memcg when necessary.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 7/7] mm: switch deferred split shrinker to list_lru
series_items.patch_summary: This patch switches the deferred split shrinker to use list_lru, which properly sets up per-node and per-cgroup lists. This improves handling of cgroups on NUMA systems by avoiding splitting large pages on other nodes when a node-restricted allocation is reclaiming memory. The change also streamlines list operations and reclaim walks, and is consistent with how other major shrinkers use list_lru.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series switches the THP (Transparent Huge Pages) shrinker to use list_lru instead of an open-coded deferred split queue. The current implementation has issues, including being non-NUMA-aware and complicated in its interactions with other code. Using list_lru fixes these problems and simplifies planned shrinker work. The patch series includes several cleanups and refactors in the list_lru code to make the conversion easier, as well as extensions to the list_lru API to allow for more control over locking scope and a convenience helper for allocating LRU heads when coming from a folio.

On Wed, Mar 18, 2026 at 9:50 PM Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org> wrote:

review_comments.raw_body:
On Mon, 16 Mar 2026 21:12:08 +0000 "Lorenzo Stoakes (Oracle)" <ljs@kernel.org> wrote:

> While the conversion of mmap hooks to mmap_prepare is underway, we wil
> encounter situations where mmap hooks need to invoke nested mmap_prepare
> hooks.
> 
> The nesting of mmap hooks is termed 'stacking'.  In order to flexibly
> facilitate the conversion of custom mmap hooks in drivers which stack, we
> must split up the existing compat_vma_mapped() function into two separate
> functions:
> 
> * compat_set_desc_from_vma() - This allows the setting of a vm_area_desc
>   object's fields to the relevant fields of a VMA.

It crashes at compat_vma_mmap, and here is what I think could be the 
potential crash path:

- compat_vma_mmap() creates struct vm_area_desc desc;
  - compat_set_desc_from_vma Doesn't initialize the struct, but instead
    modifies independent fields. I think this is where the behavior
    diverges, since before we would use the C initializer and uninitialized
    variables would be set to 0 (including ommitted ones, like
    action.success_hook or action.error_hook). But action.type = MMAP_NOTHING
  - desc.action.success_hook remains uninitialized in vfs_mmap_prepare
  - mmap_action_complete()
    - Here, We've set action.type to be MMAP_NOTHING, so we have err = 0
    - mmap_action_finish(action, vma, 0)
      - And here, since err == 0, we check action->success_hook (which has
        garbage, therefore it's nonzero) and call action->success_hook(vma)

And I think action->success_hook(vma) where success_hook is uninitialized
stack garbage gets me to where I am.

Again, I'm not too familiar with this area of the kernel, this is just
based on the quick digging that I did. And aplogies again if I'm missing
something ; -) I do think that the uninitialized members could be a problem
though.

Thank you, I hope you have a great day Lorenzo!
Joshua
